# What to look for in a private dentist in 2026 — an honest guide

Most of what's written about choosing a private dentist is written by someone trying to sell you dentistry. This is not that.

I own a private practice in Didsbury. I am therefore, by definition, someone trying to sell you dentistry. But this piece isn't pitched — it's the advice I give friends when they ask, and the advice I wish my parents had been given twenty years ago. If you read it and decide we're not the right fit for you, that's a good outcome. There are plenty of good practices in Manchester, and finding yours matters more than finding ours.

## Start with what to avoid

The red flags in dental marketing are loud if you know to listen for them.

**Promises about aesthetic outcomes.** "Perfect smile in one visit" is a marketing line, not a clinical reality. Even the simplest cosmetic treatment needs planning, healing time, and realistic expectations about what your particular teeth will do. Anyone guaranteeing an outcome has either redefined "guarantee" or is about to disappoint you.

**Price as the headline.** If the first thing a practice tells you is what something costs, you're being sold, not consulted. Good private dentistry starts with understanding you — your medical history, your bite, your gum health, what you want long-term. Price comes later, once there's an actual plan.

**Pressure to decide today.** Dental work, except emergencies, rarely needs to be decided on the day. If you're being pushed to sign a treatment plan at your first consultation, that's a red flag on someone else's cashflow.

**Heavy before-and-after marketing.** A practice whose Instagram is 90% Invisalign and whitening is telling you where its priorities are. That's not inherently bad — some people want a cosmetic-first practice. But if what you actually want is a dentist who'll catch a problem in your molars before it becomes a crown, that practice may not be it.

## Now the harder question: NHS vs private

The assumption many people arrive with is "private is better." It's more complicated than that.

NHS dentistry in the UK is rationed by what the contract pays for. A GDC-registered dentist working on the NHS is clinically capable of the same work as one in private — the difference is how much time they can spend with you and what materials the contract will fund. [General Dental Council guidance](https://www.gdc-uk.org/information-standards-guidance) requires the same clinical standards regardless of funding model.

The practical difference: an NHS dentist sees more patients per hour, so the 20-minute check-up you're used to may become 10 minutes. That's not their fault — it's the system. A good NHS dentist in a well-run NHS practice is often a better choice than a rushed "budget private" practice where the unit economics force the same time pressures without the safety net of NHS coverage.

The NHS is also under structural pressure. The [British Dental Association has reported that over 90% of NHS practices in England are not accepting new adult patients](https://bda.org/news/press-releases). If you can get NHS treatment with a dentist who knows your mouth, keep it. If you're reading this because you can't, private becomes less a luxury and more a necessity.

## What continuity of care actually means

The word "continuity" gets used so often it's lost meaning. Here's what it means in practice.

When the same dentist sees you for five years, they know your history. They know the crown on your upper-right molar was placed in 2021 and how it's worn. They know the gum recession on your lower front teeth is slow but measurable, not acute. They know you grind your teeth when you're stressed because you mentioned it once three years ago.

That context changes the appointment. A dentist who doesn't have it is forced to treat you as a case file, working from x-rays alone. A dentist who has it can ask you specifically about the crown, monitor the gum recession with a year-on-year comparison, and suggest a night guard the year you mention work stress.

This matters more as you age. In your 30s, continuity is nice. In your 50s, when you're starting to see the cumulative effects of decades of wear, restorations, and occasional dental neglect, continuity is the single biggest predictor of whether problems get caught early or late. Late is always more expensive — both financially and in terms of tooth loss.

Practically: ask who you'll see. Ask if you'll see the same dentist at every appointment. Ask how long the dentist has been at the practice. A practice with 5-year average tenure is probably set up for continuity. A practice that's had three principals in two years isn't.

## Questions to ask on a first consultation

The first consultation is a two-way interview. You're deciding if this is a dentist you'll trust for a decade. They're deciding if you're a patient they can help. Don't go in deferentially — go in curious.

Questions worth asking:

1. **"What's your approach to prevention vs intervention?"** You want someone who talks about catching things early and watchful waiting, not someone who diagnoses immediate work at every opportunity.
2. **"How do you price a treatment plan?"** Good answer: upfront, written, staged, with reasons for each stage. Bad answer: evasive, verbal, "we'll see how it goes."
3. **"Who'll I see at each appointment?"** As covered above.
4. **"What happens if I don't take a recommended treatment?"** Good answer: they explain the trade-off honestly (some things can wait, some can't). Bad answer: they push harder, or they're dismissive.
5. **"Can I see the practice hygienist?"** In a good practice, the hygienist is as important as the dentist. If the hygiene side is an afterthought, that tells you about the practice's approach to long-term oral health.

## What a good private practice looks like

Since I'm meant to be giving honest guidance and I run a practice, let me describe what I've tried to build at Meadow Lane — not as a sales pitch, but as one example of what "good" looks like so you know what to compare other practices to.

We run 30-minute check-ups because 10 minutes isn't enough to look properly. We price transparently — every treatment plan is written, staged, and costed before you commit. You see me, or one of two associates who've been here for years — not a rotating cast of locums. We push hygienist appointments hard because prevention is cheaper than crowns. And we say "no" to cosmetic cases we don't think will hold up long-term, even when the patient really wants them, because our job is your teeth in 2040, not our invoice in 2026.

You'll find a similar description on most practices' websites. What matters is whether the lived experience matches. That's what the first consultation is for — and why questions 1-5 above actually matter.

## One final thing

The "right" dentist for you is almost never the cheapest, but they're also almost never the flashiest. They're the one you trust to tell you you don't need treatment when you don't, and to tell you honestly when you do. That trust takes time to build — which is why getting the choice right the first time matters more than getting it perfect.

If you're in South Manchester and still working through this, feel free to [book a no-obligation first consultation with us](https://meadowlane-dental.co.uk/book) — 30 minutes, £45, includes a full examination and a conversation about whether we're the right fit. If we're not, I'll tell you so, and point you somewhere that is.
